text: Despite a previous thoracotomy and recurrent left lower lobe infections, a minimally invasive surgical approach was attempted.
We made a 4 cm incision at the level of 5th intercostal space between anterior axillary line and mid axillary line on the pre-existing torquotomy scar.
Widespread adhesions were encountered upon entering the left pleural cavity.
Division of the adhesions was started using a combination of an advanced energy device and monopolar coterie.
Anterior and apical adhesions were divided first.
Then, linear adhesions along the trochotomy incision were divided in a dorsal direction.
Finally, basal-paramediastinal adhesions were divided.
After all adhesions were divided, we turned our attention to division of mediastinal pleura along the anterior aspect of the pulmonary hilum.
Then we found the interlobar fissure which was also located much more cranially than usually expected.
The inferior pulmonary vein was identified and dissected.
Please note the large diameter of the vein and the unusually high location of the pulmonary hilum.
The interlobar fissure was fused and separation of the lobes was started.
Upon dissecting the interlobar fissure we found the interlobar part of the pulmonary artery.
identification and dissection of the lower lobe arterial branches was started.
Three arteries were found that were going to different parts of the lower lobe.
A powered stapler with 45 mm vascular reload was used to divide these three arteries.
Immediately upon division, another artery was discovered underneath.
Then another one was found more dorsal,
At this point, the main pulmonary artery trunk was exposed to confirm the correct interpretation of the anatomy.
After that, two lower lobe arterial branches were divided with 45 mm vascular reload.
The anterior portion of the interlobar fissure was opened with a 60 mm stapler
The sixth arterial branch for the lower lobe was discovered.
It was dissected and divided together with remaining interlobar pulmonary tissue.
We do not routinely perform 3D reconstructions for lobectomy cases.
However, we performed a reconstruction for this patient after the surgery, mainly for demonstration purposes.
This is her 3D reconstruction.
And if we look for the arteries, these are colored in blue, we can really see six separate branches for the left lower lobe.
So these are the first three branches that were divided.
And now you can see another two branches that were discovered after that.
And here is the remaining branch which was more ventral.
We can see that anatomy provided by 3D reconstruction really matches the actual surgical anatomy.
Now we continue with the operation and the previously dissected inferior pulmonary vein was encircled and divided with a vascular reload powered stapler.
After that, the only remaining lower lobe structure was the bronchus, which was divided with a thick tissue black color reload.
This choice was made due to the size of the bronchus and the patient's history of recurrent infections.
Upon division of the bronchus, the left lower lobectomy was completed.
The specimen was removed in a bag
Proper hemostasis was ensured.
The parenchymal laceration on the interlobar surface of the upper lobe was sutured with PDS 4-0 suture.
The pleural cavity was irrigated and the lung was inflated.
The volume mismatch between the remaining upper lobe of the left lung and the pleural cavity was clearly visible.
This was already predicted and discussed with the patient before the operation.
No major air leak was observed during inflation and the volume of the lung slightly increased over time.
A single 20 French chest drain was inserted through the separate incision since a prolonged air leak was expected in this case.
For this purpose we used pre-existing scar of the drain site from the previous operation.
